task_objective.description: Simulate LLM-based personas that are parameterised by human trait scores (BFI, Need for Cognition, CRT2) and then administer psychometric tests and writing tasks to those personas.
simulation_focus: Read synthetic_personas.csv and, for each row, construct one or more persona prompts that configure an LLM according to that row's demographic variables and trait scores.

Administer psychometric tests (CRT2, bCRT, NFC-18, BFI-brief) and reflection-related writing tasks to each configured persona, using configurable granularity of API calls (per item, per test, or all tests at once).
Record all LLM responses in structured JSON, automatically merged with the originating persona row, to create a single analysis-ready dataset.

data_files.synthetic_personas.csv: N=470 synthetic personas with demographic variables and target trait scores for Big Five (BFI), Need for Cognition (NFC), and CRT2. Columns include numeric scores and, where available, short human-readable descriptions.
data_files.crt2_items.yaml: YAML specification of the 4-item CRT2 form, including item text and correct answers.
data_files.bcrt_items.yaml: YAML specification of the 4-item bespoke CRT (bCRT) form, including item text and correct answers.
data_files.nfc18_items.yaml: YAML specification of the 18-item Need for Cognition short form, including item text and keyed direction (p/a).
data_files.bfi10_items.yaml: YAML specification of the 8-item BFI-brief questionnaire used in the synthetic dataset.
data_files.writing_tasks.yaml: YAML specification of 2 reflection-related writing prompts used as downstream behavioural measures.
data_files.crt_scoring_config.yaml: Optional config that encodes mapping of free-form LLM responses on CRT items to ordinal labels (0 = other error, 1 = intuitive-incorrect, 2 = correct) for automatic scoring.

trait_fidelity.description: How well simulated test scores reproduce target trait scores from synthetic_personas.csv for BFI, NFC, and CRT2.
examples: Pearson and Spearman correlations between target scores and obtained scores for each scale.
Mean absolute error (MAE) between target and obtained CRT2 scores.
Monotonicity checks that higher target trait levels yield stochastically higher test scores.

behavioural_alignment.description: Degree to which specified traits predict downstream behaviour on writing tasks.
examples: Regress LIWC-based features of writing (e.g., analytic, insight, affect) on specified CRT/NFC levels and check for theoretically expected trends.
Compare distributions of writing-task scores across coarse and granular persona conditions.

response_consistency.description: Internal reliability and stability of responses within each simulated persona.
examples: Cronbach's alpha or omega for NFC and BFI scales.
Test-retest consistency when repeating the same questionnaire for a subset of personas.
Within-session self-consistency when item order is shuffled.

administration_modes.description: Control over how many items are sent per API call.
options: per_item: one API call per item with persona prompt + single item.
per_test: one API call per test with persona prompt + all items in that test.
all_tests: one API call per persona with persona prompt + all tests and writing tasks.

shuffling_options.description: Options for randomisation to control order effects.

qualifier_scale_1_to_9.description: Linguistic qualifiers applied to low and high pole descriptors for both BFI and NFC.

coarse_numeric.description: Map continuous Big Five scores from synthetic_personas.csv (typically 1–5 with 0.5 increments) into three bins: low, neutral, high.
coarse_numeric.example: You are a person with high extraversion.

coarse_descriptive.description: Same three-way binning as coarse_numeric, but use descriptive words drawn from the markers table (serapio_goldberg_markers.csv).
coarse_descriptive.prompt_template: You are a person who is {descriptor_list}.
coarse_descriptive.example: You are a person who is friendly, talkative, and energetic.

granular_serapio.description: Nine-level shaping of each Big Five domain using Goldberg/Serapio adjective pairs and the 1–9 qualifier scale. Synthetic scores (1–5 with .5 increments) are linearly mapped to 1–9 levels.
granular_serapio.prompt_template: For the following tasks, respond as a person described as: "I am {qualified_descriptors}."
granular_serapio.qualified_descriptors_construction: For a given domain (e.g., Extraversion) and target level L, choose either low_marker(s) or high_marker(s) from the CSV depending on whether L <= 4 or L >= 6. Apply the corresponding qualifier string from qualifier_scale_1_to_9, and optionally join 2–3 markers with commas.

granular_9_level.description: Nine-level shaping of Need for Cognition based on the validated 18-item NFC short form. High and low descriptors are derived from positively and reverse-keyed items.
high_descriptors: enjoys complex rather than simple problems
likes responsibility for situations that require a lot of thinking
finds satisfaction in deliberating hard and for long periods
enjoys tasks that involve generating new solutions to problems
prefers life to be filled with puzzles and challenging questions
prefers intellectual, difficult, and important tasks
often deliberates about issues even when they do not affect them personally
low_descriptors: thinks only as hard as necessary
prefers tasks that require little thought once learned
would rather do something that requires little thought
tries to avoid situations that demand deep thinking
finds thinking effortful rather than fun
feels relief rather than satisfaction after heavy mental effort
is content when something works without understanding how or why
granular_9_level.prompt_template: For the following tasks, respond as a person described as: "I {qualified_nfc_description}."
construction_rules: Select one to three phrases from high_descriptors or low_descriptors, depending on the target level.
Apply the 1–9 qualifier scale: levels 1–4 use low_descriptors, levels 6–9 use high_descriptors, and level 5 combines both in a 'neither low nor high' clause.
Example high level (L=8): "I very much enjoy complex problems, find satisfaction in deliberating hard, and prefer intellectual, difficult tasks."
Example low level (L=2): "I very much prefer tasks that require little thought, think only as hard as necessary, and try to avoid situations that demand deep thinking."

levels_0_to_4.description: Coarse specification of CRT2-like reflective thinking ability using five behavioural levels. Each level can be invoked numerically (e.g., crt2_level=2) and via an associated description sentence.
levels_0_to_4.numeric_tag_template: This persona has a CRT2 ability level of {crt2_level} on a 0–4 scale.

level_descriptions.label: very low reflection
level_descriptions.sentence: I almost always trust my first impression, answer quickly without re-checking, and rarely notice when a question might be tricky.

level_descriptions.label: low reflection
level_descriptions.sentence: I often go with my first impression and only occasionally stop to reconsider whether it might be misleading.

level_descriptions.label: mixed reflection
level_descriptions.sentence: I sometimes pause to reconsider my first impression before answering, but I am inconsistent and often stick with the obvious answer.

level_descriptions.label: high reflection
level_descriptions.sentence: I usually pause to check whether an obvious answer could be a trap, and I am willing to change my mind after thinking things through.

level_descriptions.label: very high reflection
level_descriptions.sentence: I almost always look for hidden assumptions, carefully check for tricks, and verify my answers with calculations before responding.

levels_0_to_4.prompt_template: For the following CRT-style questions, respond as a person described as: "{crt_sentence}"
usage_notes: The simulator should allow two modes: (a) numeric-only specification, passing crt2_level as a variable, and (b) full descriptive specification using the corresponding sentence.
No instruction should mention scores, correctness, or 'being wrong'; the sentences describe thinking style only.
For now, no optimisation loop over prompts is included; the simulator simply instantiates these fixed descriptions per level.
